costs: Legal/constitutional costs of revisiting maintainer exemptions, given prior Curia rulings protecting church-school discretion
Administrative cost of redesigning zoning and monitoring systems nationally amid a contracting school network (1,200+ settlements already without institutions)
content.equity_impact: Likely positive on segregation specifically, since it targets the structural amplifiers (choice commuting, maintainer exemptions, geographic concentration) identified as operating independently of track-age selection; however, does not address the SES/tutoring-driven access advantage within tracks themselves.
expected_benefits: Targets segregation drivers described as requiring 'much more radical' structural interventions beyond the track question itself
Politically may be more tractable than track-age reform since it does not touch the symbolically salient 6-/8-year structure directly
Complements rather than conflicts with either preserving or reforming the tracks
content.goal: Address the structural drivers of segregation identified as independent of the tracking mechanism itself — school-choice commuting, maintainer exemptions, geographic concentration — while leaving the 6-/8-year track age and admissions criteria untouched.

implementation_steps.action: Review and potentially narrow legal exemptions allowing church/private maintainers to bypass anti-discrimination admissions provisions
implementation_steps.actor: Ministry of Education / Ministry of Justice
implementation_steps.timeline: Year 1-2, requires legislative and possibly constitutional-court engagement

implementation_steps.action: Redesign catchment/zoning rules to limit cross-municipal 'choice commuting' correlated with inequality, informed by settlement-level data
implementation_steps.actor: Klebelsberg Centre and municipal planning bodies

implementation_steps.action: Introduce national desegregation targets and monitoring modeled on the 2000s administrative rules, avoiding the mid-decade stagnation observed previously
implementation_steps.timeline: Year 1 onward, with periodic review to prevent stagnation

mechanisms: Geographic settlement patterns independently produce stratification (village pupils funneled to vocational schools regardless of academic tracking), suggesting zoning/network levers operate through a distinct causal channel from track admissions
Higher inter-school choice commuting correlates with stronger inter-school inequality, indicating that restricting or structuring choice/commuting could reduce segregation without touching track age

2015 Curia exemption permitting segregated Roma-only schools under Church-maintainer status indicates a specific legal lever (review of anti-discrimination exemptions) distinct from admissions-exam reform
risks: Prior administrative desegregation rules from the 2000s only produced temporary reductions before stagnating, suggesting this lever alone may have limited durability
Legal challenge risk given the Curia's prior exemption ruling for Greek Catholic Church schools
Given demographic contraction and centralization under KLIK curtailing municipal planning autonomy, local adaptation capacity for zoning changes is constrained
content.title: Structural desegregation without touching track age (choice and zoning reform)
